results.title: 2025 HR Tech M&A Activity: Mega-Deals, Agentic AI, and the ...
results.author: Ben Eubanks
results.text:
2025 HR Tech M&A Activity: Mega-Deals, Agentic AI, and the Learning Shakeup – Lighthouse Research & Advisory

## 2025 HR Tech M&A Activity: Mega-Deals, Agentic AI, and the Learning Shakeup

2025 was a year of big swings in HR technology M&A.

Private equity wrote checks measured in billions. Platform vendors went on acquisition sprees. And the learning technology market saw consolidation that will reshape how organizations think about skills development for years to come.

For instance, through Q3 2025 alone, Drake Star recorded 650+ HR Tech transactions globally with $22.3 billion in disclosed invested capital. Safe to say the market is not slowing down.

A few things stood out this year.

Private equity set the tone. Thoma Bravo’s $12.3 billion take-private of Dayforce was the headline, but it wasn’t alone. Paychex grabbed Paycor for $4.1 billion. HG doubled down on P&I with a $5.9 billion secondary stake. When PE firms write checks this big, they expect returns, which usually means tuck-in acquisitions and a lot of “synergy” (cost-cutting) talk. If you are a standalone vendor without a clear path to profitability or acquisition, this trend should keep you up at night.

Learning technology finally got its moment. I have been covering this space for years, and I cannot remember a single year with this much M&A activity in learning and skills. Coursera and Udemy are merging into a $2.5 billion company. Workday paid roughly $1.1 billion for Sana. ETU and Realizeit combined to form Skillwell. Skills intelligence is becoming the connective tissue between hiring, mobility, and development. The harder question is how organizations measure whether any of this actually works.

Workday made a statement. Three acquisitions in rapid succession tells you something. Paradox for frontline hiring. Sana for AI-powered learning. FlowiseAI for agent builder capabilities. Workday wants to be a system of action, not just a system of record. Whether they can integrate all of this without stumbling is another question entirely.

And yes, agentic AI started showing up in actual products. Not just pitch decks. Phenom rolled out multiple hiring agents. LinkedIn launched its Hiring Assistant. A handful of vendors shipped AI that executes tasks rather than just surfacing insights. Expect every vendor to claim “agentic” capabilities in 2026 (and for too few to actually deliver).

## Key 2025 HR Technology Mergers and Acquisitions

Here are some of the notable deals from the past year.

### Talent Acquisition

- Workday acquires Paradox: Workday completed its acquisition of Paradox, the conversational AI company known for its work with high-volume employers like McDonald’s, FedEx, and 7-Eleven. This gives Workday a native AI-powered front door for frontline and hourly hiring. Paradox has quietly become one of the most deployed conversational hiring tools in the market, and embedding it into Workday changes the competitive landscape for enterprise TA suites.
- SAP acquires S

results.title: Lighthouse introduces direct hotel booking through ChatGPT
results.author: Anwesha Pattanaik
results.text:
Lighthouse introduces direct hotel booking through ChatGPT 

 

Travellers can follow one-click links from ChatGPT directly to hotel websites to complete reservations. Credit: Andrey_Popov / Shutterstock.com.

Lighthouse has launched The Hotels Network app, a direct booking application for hotels now available within ChatGPT.

The app integrates with ChatGPT to provide travellers with real-time information and direct access to hotel reservations.

 

### Go deeper with GlobalData

Reports

#### Travel Apps - Thematic Research

##### Go deeper with GlobalData

The gold standard of business intelligence.

Find out more

 

#### Discover B2B Marketing That Performs

Combine business intelligence and editorial excellence to reach engaged professionals across 36 leading media platforms.

Find out more

The app enables hotels to present their own property information, live rates, and booking links directly to ChatGPT’s global user base.

By integrating with Lighthouse’s Connect AI engine, hotels can supply verified content and up-to-date pricing directly into AI-assisted travel conversations.

The service is accessible to hotels of all sizes worldwide through a flat fee subscription model that does not charge booking commissions.

With more than 800 million users interacting with ChatGPT globally, the channel provides hotels with access to a substantial audience seeking accommodation recommendations.

Until now, online travel agencies such as Booking.com and Expedia have established partnerships as launch partners within ChatGPT, leaving individual hotels without comparable direct access.

For travellers, using the app means that hotel recommendations in ChatGPT are based on official descriptions, property-verified photographs, and live rates provided by the hotels themselves.

This approach replaces summaries generated from third-party listings or scraped online content.

Travellers can follow one-click links from ChatGPT directly to hotel websites to complete reservations.

The app also provides hoteliers with tools to manage their property narrative and respond to specific traveller inquiries within AI platforms.

Through Connect AI technology built on the Model Context Protocol (MCP) standard, properties can display unique features and answer questions based on structured real-time data.

This allows responses in ChatGPT conversations to reflect accurate amenities, guest experience details, and other relevant information beyond price or star ratings.

Lighthouse’s direct booking head Juanjo Rodriguez said: “Properties can now control how they’re discovered, tell their own story and surface their own rates, all inside the conversations where trip planning starts. This isn’t an incremental improvement. It’s a new distribution channel.”

The Hotels Network app is currently accessible via the ChatGPT App Directory.

Participation does not require changes to hotel websites or management systems; both independent properties
